coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots enable dendrite-free zinc ion battery
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hao
Guo, Ruiting
Li, Shuo
Liu, Cheng
Li, Haoyang
Zou, Guoqiang
Hu, Jiugang
Hou, Hongshuai
Ji, Xiaobo
coredata.dc:description: The practical application of aqueous Zn-ion batteries has been largely plagued by the poor reversibility of Zn anode, mainly associated with the dendritic growth and interfacial parasitic reaction. Hereby, electrochemically stable Zn anode is successfully designed by manipulating nucleation process through the introduction of hydrophilic graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Notably, the enhanced binding effect of GQDs with Zn2+ is aroused from the lower electronegativity of GQDs, which is conducive to accelerating uniform Zn deposition, resulting in the robust Zn anode without dendrites. Concomitantly, interfacial hydrogen bonds are excited by the GQDs with oxygen-containing groups, which is beneficial for mitigating water-induced side reaction and improving Zn ion reaction kinetics. Greatly, polarization voltage of symmetric cells is decreased from 80 to 50 mV at 0.8 mA cm−2, presenting a prolonged lifespan of 2200 h. As a result, the assembled ZIBs with vanadium cathode exhibit superior performance with capacity of 164.3 mAh g−1 after 600 cycles at 1 A g−1. Given this, this elaborate work might inaugurate a potential avenue for rationally tuning the electrode/electrolyte interface evolution towards advanced aqueous batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc ion batteries
Zinc anode
Dendrites
Graphene quantum dots
Electrolyte additive

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Calculation models of Zn ion absorbed on different oxygen-function groups and corresponding binding energy (a). Schematic depiction of the effect towards suppressing dendrites growth (b).

TEM (a, b) and HRTEM (insert in b) images of as-prepared GQDs. AFM image (c) of the GQDs on the mica substrate and corresponding height profile of the origin line A-B (d). XRD patterns of the pristine graphite and GQDs (e). Raman spectra of the pristine graphite and GQDs (f). FTIR spectra of GQDs (g). Deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (h) and O1s (i).

Long-term cycling profiles of the Zn||Zn symmetric cells with and without GQDs additive at 0.8 mA cm−2 and the corresponding voltage profiles (a), 2.0 mA cm−2 and the corresponding voltage profiles (b). Rate performance (c) and the corresponding voltage hysteresis at different current densities (d).

Optical microscope photos of zinc anode without GQDs (a) and with GQDs (b) after 10 cycles at 0.8 mA cm−2 in Zn symmetric cells. SEM images of zinc anode after platting 30 min at 10 mA cm−2 without GQDs (c1, c2) and with GQDs (c3, c4). SEM images of zinc foil after 20 cycles at 0.8 mA cm−2 without GQDs (d1, d2) and with GQDs (d3, d4). Confocal luminescence images of Zn deposition layer without GQDs (e, g) and with GQDs (f, h) at 0.5 mA cm−2 with an area capacity of 0.5 mAh cm−2 in bright field (e, f) and dark field (g, h). EPMA mapping images of Zn foil electrodes after 20 cycles at 0.8 mA cm−2 in normal electrolyte (i, j) and GQDs-containing electrolyte (k, l).

EIS spectra of the Zn symmetric cells after 50 cycles (a). Nucleation overpotentials test of zinc ion using Zn||Cu cells at 1 mA cm−2 (b). Chronoamperograms of Zn anodes using Zn||SS cells (c). Linear polarization curves showing the corrosion rate of zinc anode using three-electrode system (d). XRD patterns of zinc anode after cycling tests at 0.8 mA cm−2 with a deposited amount of 0.2 mAh cm−2 (e). CV curves of Zn plating/stripping at 0.2 mV s−1 (f).

CV curves (a) and EIS (b) of Zn||NVO with and without GQDs. Long-cycling performance of Zn||NVO with and without GQDs at 0.5 A g−1 (c). Rate capabilities of the Zn||NVO at different current densities from 0.1 to 2 A g−1 (d). Self-discharge of Zn||NVO without (e) and with (f) GQDs. Long-time cycling performance of Zn||NVO with or without GQDs at 1 A g−1 (h). SEM of zinc anode after 600 cycles at 1 A g−1 without (i) and with GQDs (j).

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots enable dendrite-free zinc ion battery

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The practical application of aqueous Zn-ion batteries has been largely plagued by the poor reversibility of Zn anode, mainly associated with the dendritic growth and interfacial parasitic reaction. Hereby, electrochemically stable Zn anode is successfully designed by manipulating nucleation process through the introduction of hydrophilic graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Notably, the enhanced binding effect of GQDs with Zn2+ is aroused from the lower electronegativity of GQDs, which is conducive to accelerating uniform Zn deposition, resulting in the robust Zn anode without dendrites. Concomitantly, interfacial hydrogen bonds are excited by the GQDs with oxygen-containing groups, which is beneficial for mitigating water-induced side reaction and improving Zn ion reaction kinetics. Greatly, polarization voltage of symmetric cells is decreased from 80 to 50 mV at 0.8 mA cm−2, presenting a prolonged lifespan of 2200 h. As a result, the assembled ZIBs with vanadium cathode exhibit superior performance with capacity of 164.3 mAh g−1 after 600 cycles at 1 A g−1. Given this, this elaborate work might inaugurate a potential avenue for rationally tuning the electrode/electrolyte interface evolution towards advanced aqueous batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: With increased demand for novel safe and sustainable electric energy storage (EES) [1–4], aqueous zinc ion batteries (ZIBs) have been gradually considered as prospective alternatives for next generation batteries due to their cost eﬀectiveness, excellent ion conductivity, non-flammability and uncomplex operating condition [5–7]. Although metallic Zn holds outstanding advantages with high gravimetric and volumetric capacity (820 mAh g−1 and 5855 mAh cm−3), appropriate redox potential (−0.76 V vs standard hydrogen electrode), there are still some severe problems in the zinc anode, such as notorious dendrites growth, the formation of byproduct, and zinc corrosion, highly limiting commercialization of Zn anode [8,9]. Among these disturbing problems, the formation of dendrites during cycling is an urgent issue because continuous protrusion growth could accelerate concomitant side reaction and pierce separators resulting in short-circuiting batteries [10,11]. A homogeneous surface texture of Zn anodes during plating/stripping process is thus considered to be fatal to realize longer cycling time and higher cycling stability [12].

Similar to lithium dendrites [13–15], the initial formation of zinc protuberance occurs on the zinc anode due to inhomogeneous electric field distribution resulting from “tip effect”, which causes zinc ion concentration polarization and leads to further uneven zinc deposition on the zinc anode [16]. To alleviate the growth of dendrites, a variety of strategies, have been adopted to extend cycling lifespan and improve cycling stability of zinc anode during plating/stripping process such as Zn surface modification [17–19], electrolyte engineering [20–22] and three-dimensional (3D) nanostructure [23–25]. For example, liquid Ga-In alloy coating, a protective inner layer was proposed to impede the dendrite growth via guide uniform Zn2+ ions flux. The coated zinc anode exhibited long cycling lifetime (1200 h) at 1 mA cm−2 and 0.1 mAh cm−2. However, the complicated technology limits their large-scale practical applications. Compared with planar Zn, the 3D Zn anode presents enormous merits in inhibiting dendrites growth, with advantage of less zinc anode polarization and smaller Zn nucleation overpotential. However, this approach meets same puzzle for practical applications. Electrolyte additives have been widely considered as a cost-effective and efficient method to stabilize Zn metal anode. Recently, it was found that a small additional amount (2 vol%) of diethyl ether (Et2O) [26], could hinder further deposition of Zn2+ to initial tips through preferential adsorption on tips of zinc anode, thus reducing dendrite growth rate. Meanwhile, the Zn symmetric cells in the presence of Et2O with smaller voltage fluctuation and charge transfer resistance can stably operate for 250 h at 0.2 mA cm−2. In addition, a low-cost polyacrylamide (PAM) with ketone units was introduced into electrolyte [27], thus uniform zinc deposition with extended cycling life (280 h) at a discharge depth of 80% was obtained due to better zinc affinity and reduced nucleation overpotential. The PAM, with a strong absorption to zinc by zinc-oriented acyl group served as guiding intermediary to balance electric field distribution and inhibit dendrites formation, which is in favor of enhancing cycling stability. However, developing novel additive with high stability and long-term cycling life is still an urgent task, especially at high current densities.

Due to abundant functional groups, quantum size (less than 10 nm), photoluminescence property and low-toxicity, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) attract many attentions as a kind of novel zero-dimensional carbon materials in various fields [28,29], such as biological imaging and diagnosis [30], sensor [31], catalysis and EES [32,33]. Herein, a practical and effective approach was proposed to inhibit the growth of zinc dendrite by using GQDs as electrolyte additive for high performance aqueous ZIBs. In this work, GQDs was prepared from cheap graphite powder by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. DFT calculation and experimental results synergistically confirm that the GQDs additive could stabilize zinc metal anode by lowering nucleation energy barrier and regulating Zn2+ ions distribution during cycling process in comparison with pristine electrolyte. The GQDs are capable of adsorbing on the high-potential tips of Zn anode with stronger adsorption between Zn2+ and oxygen-contain groups of GQDs, thus obtaining reduced nucleation overpotential and dendrites-free zinc anode. The electrochemical inert GQDs also reduce the reactivity of water due to stronger intermolecular interaction with water via hydrogen bond, thus improving anti-corrosion current of zinc and alleviating the formation of byproducts (Zn4(OH)6SO4.4 H2O) [34,35]. Therefore, profiting from GQDs electrolyte additive, the zinc anode exhibits a longer cycling lifetime (2200 h) at 0.8 mA cm−2 with a lower voltage hysteresis of 50 mV, which would highly suppress the formation of dendrites during repeated deposition/dissolution process. When assembled ZIBs by using NaxV2O5·nH2O cathode, Zn anode and electrolyte with GQDs additive, at the current density of 1 A g−1, a capacity of 164.3 mAh g−1 was observed after 600 cycles, which confirmed the markedly improved electrochemical performance.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared via a simple hydrothermal method. The graphite powder (0.8 g) was added into a mixture of concentrated HNO3 (12 mL) and H2SO4 (36 mL). The mixed solution was stirred for 10 min and then was sonicated for 1 h until graphite was evenly dispersed in the solution, followed by hydrothermal reaction at 90 °C for 11 h in sealed 100 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the black mixture solution was diluted with 20 mL deionized water (DI). The PH was adjusted to neutral with Na2CO3 and NaOH. The as-prepared red brown mixture after centrifugation and filtration was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 4 days, followed by freeze-dried to obtain brown and fluffy final product (GQDs).

The NaxV2O5·nH2O (NVO) cathode materials were fabricated by a facile hydrothermal method [36]. 2 mmol commercial V2O5, 1 mmol Na3C6H5O7.2 H2O and 30 mL DI were added into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, respectively. Subsequently, the yellow solution was stirred vigorously for 30 min at room temperature, followed by heating at 160 °C for 48 h. After cooling to room temperature, the black solution was washed with ethanol and water for several times. Finally, the obtained blue precipitate was dried in at 80 °C for 10 h.

Raman spectra were tested by a Renishaw in Via spectrometer using an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. The microscopic morphology of GQDs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, Shanghai Chenmai Co.) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements were carried out on a Bruker multimode-8 scanning probe microscope. The crystalline patterns of prepared samples were recorded by X-ray diffraction using a Rigaku Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation of 1.5406 Å (the scan rate of 15° min −1). The detailed compositions were determined by using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyzer (XPS) (Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi) and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (AVTA-TAR, 370). The contact angles θ between the Zn anode and electrolyte without and with GQDs were tested on a Contact Angle Goniometer (SDC-100 China, Dongguan). The optical images of zinc anode were observed by Digital Microscope KH-7700. And the surface morphologies of anode were evaluated by the scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-7610 F Plus) equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS, OXFORD X-Max). The fluorescence images of zinc metal anode with and without GQDs were recorded on a confocal laser scanning microscope (Leica TCS SP8, Germany) under the excitation of 458 nm with a 10x objective lens. Electron probe microanalysis was carried out on a JXA-8530 F PLUS instrument with wavelength-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (WDS) characterization.

The coin cells (CR2016) were assembled for Zn||Cu half cells, Zn||SS (stain steel) half cells, Zn||Zn symmetric cells, Zn||NVO full cells in the air atmosphere using glass fiber as separator and 2 M ZnSO4 electrolyte with or without additives. 0.2 mol ZnSO4.7 H2O (57.51 g) and 40 mg GQDs were dissolved in DI water and transferred into 100 mL volumetric flask to obtain the 2 M electrolyte with 0.4 g L−1 GQDs additives in Zn||Zn symmetric cell. Other different concentrations of GQDs (0.2, 0.8, and 1.2 g L−1) were prepared in the same process. If not mentioned, the concentration of GQDs in 2 M ZnSO4 is 0.4 g L−1. CV analysis of Zn||Zn symmetric cells at 0.1 mV s−1 with an electrochemical potential window from − 0.1–0.1 V, linear polarization measurement, chronoamperometry method, ex-situ electrochemical impedance spectroscopy with a frequency range of 100k–0.01 Hz were all performed on electrochemical workstation (CHI660E). Tafel curves were analyzed with voltage window between − 300–300 mV vs the open circuit potential at scan rate of 1 mV s−1 in Zn||Zn symmetric cells and a typical three-electrode system with zinc foil as the working electrode, stainless steel mesh as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, respectively. The galvanostatic charge/discharge tests with a voltage window from 0.4 to 1.5 V for NVO were measured by using a Neware BTS4000 test system (LANHE, China) after resting 2 h. The NVO cathodes were obtained by mixing 70 wt% active materials, 10 wt% binder polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) and 20 wt% conductive carbon (Super P) in N-methyl pyrrolidinone (NMP). Then the acquired slurries were coated on the stainless-steel mesh with the active materials about 0.8–1.5 mg cm−2. And then as-prepared electrodes were dried under vacuum at 100 °C for 10 h.

Density The GO-graphite substrate model was designed in our study as the follows: Perfect graphite with two layers was constructed as the basic model, then three kinds of functional groups (-COO, -OH, -O-) were anchored separately on the surface of this model to form the final GO-graphite substrate model, which have been commonly considered from our experimental results. Subsequently, one ionic Zn was manually placed on three functional regions of the GO-graphite substrate model. Finally, 3 initial systems were built in a triclinic simulation box, of which the dimensions were x = 14.57 Å, y = 14.57 Å, and z = 20.40 Å, respectively. In the meantime, a substrate structure of Zn (001) surface with four layers was also designed in this work, to study the adsorption behavior of Zn ion on the Zn (001) surface.

All density functional theory (DFT) calculation was carried out with the Dmol3 program package in the Materials Studios package of Accelrys Inc to obtain the binding energy between Zn2+ ions with each functional group. The DNP version 4.4 basis set was used while the core electrons of Zn element was treated by DFT semi-core preudopots (DSPP) method. A semi-empirical van der Waals correction accounted for the dispersion was included through the use of the DFT-D method of Grimme. Solvation effects were calculated using COSMO solvation model for all the systems with water as solvent. The binding energy ( E
b ) of Zn ion on two substrates’ surfaces were calculated by the following equation:
(1)
E b=E Zn-substrate-(E Zn+E substrate)
where E
Zn and E
substrate respectively represent the energies of single Zn ion and one empty substrate, and the E
Zn-substrate is the total energy of the complex.

The GQDs with abundant oxygen-contain groups, can exhibit good water solubility and stronger coordination between Zn2+ and GQDs, making it possible for utilization as additive in aqueous Zn-ion storage system. The interaction between different oxygen-contain groups (zincophilic sites) of GQDs and Zn2+ was explored with the help of density functional theory (DFT) method. To make it easier to calculate, one ionic Zn was separately placed by three kinds of functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxy and ether group), and the simplified DFT model structures of adsorption energy are displayed in Fig. 1a. It is acknowledged that weak adsorption behavior between Zn metal and Zn ion could give rise to a rush and uneven Zn deposition, resulting in severe dendrites growth. [27,37,38] Two kinds of adsorption stabilization sites (Fig. S1, Support information) are vividly displayed on the surface of Zn (001), the bridging point of two Zn atoms on the surface (side 1) and central site of four Zn atoms on the surface (side 2) shows relatively low binding energy of −1.44 eV (side 1) and −1.50 eV (side 2), which indicates the weaker adsorption of Zn2+ on the pure Zn surface. What’s more, when comparing to that of bare Zn, there is stronger adsorption of Zn2+ on carboxyl, hydroxy and ether group with the binding energy of − 2.63, − 2.19 and − 1.78 eV, respectively, which is mainly attributed to the fact that oxygen-contain function groups as electron-rich Lewis base can strongly interact with the electron-deficit Lewis acidic Zn2+. Therefore, based on DFT analysis above, GQDs exhibit a stronger adsorption of Zn2+ by the rich-oxygen groups, which makes GQDs as a medium to guide homogeneous Zn deposition during repeated cycling process. The possible mechanism of GQDs in electroplating process of zinc anode was elucidated in Fig. 1b. Zinc ions are randomly distributed at the solid-liquid interface in the GQDs-free electrolyte. Some small zinc deposits are at first generated on the uneven surface of zinc foil, which serve as the rich-charge center in the electric field and further amplify the growth of zinc dendrites via continuing deposition on those sharp ends. When adopting the GQDs as electrolyte additive, the GQDs preferentially absorbed on the Zn surface and dissolved in electrolyte, could regulate zinc ions by oxygen-containing groups with stronger binding energy to induce the uniform zinc deposition. The lower nucleation barrier is conductive to increasement of nucleation sites, effectively suppressing the growth of the zinc dendrites. Therefore, zinc tends to grow uniformly and eventually forming a smooth plating surface.

The GQDs were prepared via a simple hydrothermal method involving strong acid and graphite powder. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the GQDs are shown in Fig. 2a and b, large numbers of GQDs derived from graphite with diameter of 1.5–3 nm could be easily observed and uniformly dispersed. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inserted in Fig. 2b) indicates a great crystallinity of the GQDs, revealing the transformation of graphite to GQDs after hydrothermal treatment [39]. In addition, as can be seen from Fig. 2c, the texture morphology of the GQDs is detected by the atomic force microscope (AFM). The height curve of Line A-B exhibited in Fig. 2d shows that the thickness of the as-prepared GQDs is between 0.4 and 1.6 nm, corresponding to 1–3 graphene layers [40]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the graphite and GQDs are displayed in Fig. 2e, and the peak at 26.54° of graphite disappears after acid oxidation, indicating the structure of graphite was destroyed and the as-synthesized GQDs were successfully exfoliated from graphite. In addition, the Raman spectrum was adopted to explore the graphitic nature spectra of graphite and as-prepared GQDs. It clearly shows that there are two obvious peaks at about 1362 cm−1 and 1585 cm−1 from Fig. 2f, consistent with the G-band (well-ordered graphite) and D-band (disordered carbon), respectively. Furthermore, there exists a better deal of defects in GQDs than in graphite, which offers enough active site for Zn2+ ions to guide uniform homogeneous nucleation [41,42]. The surface functional groups of GQDs were identified by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR). Obviously, as shown in Fig. 2g, the peaks at 1105 and 1596 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibrations of C-O and O-CO groups [43], and a broad peak at 3394 cm−1 is characteristically stretching vibration of hydroxyl group from GQDs. Therefore, it ensures the formation abundant oxygen-contain functional groups after oxidation exfoliation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) test was also carried out to analyze detailed composition of the GQDs. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2h) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.8, 286.3 and 287.8 eV, corresponding to C-C/CC, C-O and O-CO bonds, respectively [44,45]. What’s more, the peak obtained through deconvolution of the O 1 s XPS spectra (Fig. 2i) could reflect different O species. As displayed in the O 1 s XPS spectra, four types of peaks at 530.94, 531.8, 533.26 and 535.25 eV, are attributed to O-CO, C-O, C-OH and H2O, respectively [43,46]. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. S2 (Support Information) GQDs have good water-solubility in 2 M ZnSO4 as color of electrolyte gradually deepens from reddish to black with increasing concentration of GQDs. In addition, the GQDs present pale yellow when illuminated by ultraviolet lamps. It is noted that GQDs are still well dispersed in the 2 M ZnSO4 electrolyte without precipitation after long standing time (more than one month). The GQDs with functionalized hydroxyl and carbonyl groups were successfully prepared according to characteristic results above, which manifests that the GQDs served as electrolyte additive have ability to coordinate with Zn2+ to reduce nucleation overpotential resulting dendrites-free Zn deposition [47].

To prove the above-mentioned hypothesis, electrolyte solutions with various amount of GQDs additive were prepared, and the electrochemical performance was examined by Zn||Zn symmetric cells with different concentrations. Long-time cycling performance with GQDs concentration of 0.2–1.2 g L−1 at current density of 0.8 mA cm−2 and 2.0 mA cm−2 are shown in Figs. S3 and S4. Impressively, the 0.4 g L−1 of GQDs displays a more stable voltage profile with a longer cycling lifetime ( Fig. 3a and b). It is noted that the larger voltage polarization will appear due to instability of zinc anode at higher and lower concentration of GQDs and then result in the failure of batteries. In addition, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out to explore the performance distinction at different concentration in Zn symmetric cells (Fig. S5). The 0.4 g L−1 of GQDs shows the lowest interfacial resistance (Table S1), and it is mainly ascribed to the fact that a lower concentration could not totally cover all tips and meanwhile the higher one may provide additional coverage to constrain the migration of Zn2+. Therefore, the 0.4 g L−1 of GQDs would be the optimal concentration. The voltage-time curves with GQDs additive reveal smaller voltage fluctuation (50 mV) in comparison with that of normal electrolyte (80 mV) at a current density of 0.8 mA cm−2 and areal capacity of 0.2 mAh cm−2 due to a low energy barrier for metal nucleation and lower interface impedance, manifesting the higher reversibility during Zn plating/stripping process. After 55 h of stripping/plating, a sudden and profound polarization increasement is monitored in the Zn symmetric cells without GQDs. While the Zn electrodes with GQDs additive works well within a test period of 2200 h with small polarization (inserted in Fig. 3a). Specifically, the cycling life of the Zn symmetrical cells could be prolonged more than 40 times via GQDs additive strategy. Meanwhile, reduced voltage hysteresis and extended period (up to 1800 h with 50 mV) have been obtained with GQDs at 2.0 mAh cm−2 (Fig. 3b), which is significantly better than the GQDs-free electrolyte (50 h together with 100 mV). As for the zinc metal plating/stripping capacity in each cycle is increased to 1 mAh cm−2 at 1 mA cm−2, the Zn||Zn symmetric cell with GQDs enables stable operation for as long as 500 h with a lower voltage hysteresis, while normal electrolyte only lasts approximately 50 h with a high overpotential of 80 mV (Fig S6). The performance comparison of galvanostatic Zn stripping/plating in Zn||Zn symmetric cells is shown in Table S2. The current density, capacity and cycling lifespan of three key factors can be used as indicators to evaluate modified strategies. The cycling performance in our work shows competitive advantage in recently reported Zn anode via electrolyte engineering.

The rate performances of symmetric cells at different current densities were displayed in Fig. 3c. It is observed that Zn symmetric cells with GQDs deliver stable voltage profiles with a lower voltage polarization at 0.1, 0.3, 0.5. 0.8 and 1.0 mA cm−2 in comparison with the case of pure electrolyte. When the current density is increased from 0.1 to 1.0 mA cm−2, the cells with GQDs exhibit a steadily increasing voltage hysteresis of 31, 36, 41, 46 and 50 mV, respectively. Furthermore, when the current density dropped back to 0.3 mA cm−2 after 200 cycles, the voltage hysteresis can be recovered to 43 mV, suggesting a high stability in the presence of GQDs additive due to the fact that the adsorbed GQDs can regulate the distribution of Zn2+ ions and reduce nucleation barrier. However, the cell without GQDs presents higher voltage hysteresis and worse rate performance, and the cell broke down when current density returned to 0.3 mA cm−2 due to slower electrode-interface reaction kinetics. In addition, the wetting ability of electrolyte on the zinc anode is crucial for distribution of charge. Therefore, the contact angle of electrolyte on the bare zinc anode was measured in ambient environment. The contact angle changs from 108.2° to 91.4° (Fig. S7) when normal electrolyte is replaced with GQDs electrolyte indicating a better zincophilic character endowed by GQDs additive [48], which is consistent with DFT results.

The surface morphology of Zn anode at 0.8 mA cm−2 after 10 cycles was first detected by optical microscopy. It is clear to see the uneven and dendritic surface without GQDs ( Fig. 4a) in contrast to smooth and dendrites-free texture with GQDs (Fig. 4b). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was employed to investigate the external morphology of the platting Zn in two-electrode system with zinc Zn foil as work electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode. The unpolished pristine zinc foil can be seen from Fig. S8. As shown in Fig. 4c1 and c2, the deposited Zn surface exhibits massive sheet-like zinc dendrites without the assistance of GQDs, but relatively smoother zinc platting layer could be observed when using the electrolyte solution with GQDs (Fig. 4 c3 and c4), indicating the GQDs served as the nucleation site to effectively inhibit the growth of dendrites. To further verify the practical application of GQDs, the surface of zinc anode with and without GQDs was analyzed in symmetric cells during repeated cycling process. The microtopography of Zn anode surface after 20th cycled deposition with relatively high current density of 0.8 mA cm−2 for 0.2 mAh cm−2 are presented in Fig. 4 d1-d4. It is clearly to observe that the Zn piles up and evolves into uneven huge blocks by numerous aggregated nanosheets (Fig. 4 d1 and d2), which fully cover the zinc anode cycled in the normal electrolyte and may result in the persistent growth of dendrites and penetrating through separator. On the contrary, on the surface of zinc anode with GQDs, deposited Zn plates are parallel to the substrates with dense and uniform Zn surface and no obvious dendrites formation (Fig. 4d3 and d4), manifesting triumphant suppression of maniacal dendrites. In addition, the morphological evolution was further examined after 20 cycles at 2.0 mA cm−2 (Fig. S98). By-products and uneven Zn plating with plate-like dendritic growth appear on the zinc anode in the GQDs-free electrolyte (Fig. S9a). Apparently, zinc anode with GQDs displays smooth edge with little dendrites and by-products (Fig. S9b), indicating the alleviation of by-products and critical dendrite formation, which is of great help for improving cycling stability and reducing polarization potential.

The confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) was further carried out to verify the existence of GQDs by the unique fluorescent properties of GQDs. There is almost no fluorescence effect on the zinc anode with pristine electrolyte at 0.5 mA cm−2 for 0.5 mAh cm−2 in Zn symmetric cells (Fig. S10). Quite oppositely, the obvious and uniform yellow fluorescent signals on the zinc deposition are observed in the presence of GQDs at 0.5 mA cm−2 for 0.5, 1.0 and 2.0 mAh cm−2, respectively. In addition, the fluorescence on the Zn anode becomes weaker along with increasing Zn deposition capacity, which is ascribed to the fact that adsorbed GQDs are covered by deposited Zn, indicating fluorescent signals derive from GQDs on Zn anode rather than residual GQDs in the electrolyte. Meanwhile, deposited zinc foil in normal electrolyte at 0.5 mA cm−2 for 0.5 mAh cm−2 was soaked in GQDs-containing electrolyte for 30 min. After washing with water and ethanol to remove external electrolyte, no obvious fluorescent signal on zinc foil is observed on account of excellent water solubility of GQDs (Fig. 4e and g). There is still numerous yellow fluorescence signal on zinc deposition with GQDs electrolyte after same treatment (Fig. 4f and h) manifesting the GQDs co-deposition on zinc anode. The GQDs of adsorbed on zinc anode and dissolved in electrolyte are avail to restraining dendritic growth via inducing uniform Zn deposition behavior. The surface texture and element changes of Zn anode after continuous plating and stripping were also studied by electron probe microanalysis (EPMA). As shown in Fig. 4i-l and Figs. S11-S13, compared to the Zn electrode in pristine electrolyte, zinc electrode with GQDs exhibits a more homogeneous distribution and higher concentration (up to 93% versus 73% in pure ZnSO4 electrolyte) of the Zn element. And the S element is 0.07% versus 2.56%, respectively, indicating the alleviation of Zn metal corrosion (formation of Zn4SO4(OH)6·xH2O) during the plating/stripping process.

Notably, the impedance spectroscopy measurement was performed after different cycles ( Figs. 5a and S14), and the charge transfer resistance of the cell with GQDs is much smaller than that of the case without GQDs (Table S3), indicating fast electron transport capability. Benefiting from the fast electron/mass transport, the reaction between electrons and Zn2+ quickly occurs at the solid-liquid interface, which ameliorates the electrodeposition process of zinc with low polarization and interface resistance. Apart from enhancing the kinetics of Zn2+ plating/stripping, the adsorbed GQDs also play an important role in modifying Zn nucleation and uniformizing Zn deposition. The value of the nucleation overpotential reflects the energy barrier of heterogeneous nucleation during the initial deposition, which is defined as the difference between the voltage tip and the stable voltage platform. The nucleation overpotentials of Zn2+ in Zn||Cu half-cells at different current densities were measured to explain the role of GQDs in regulating Zn nucleation behavior (Fig. 5b). Obviously, the GQDs-containing electrolyte only exhibits 36.5 mV nucleation overpotential at 1.0 mA cm−2, which is much lower than that (44.9 mV) of normal electrolyte. Furthermore, such obvious difference is still observed even at lower current densities (Fig. S15), reflecting that the lower nucleation energy barrier and more uniform nucleation of Zn are achieved via GQDs additive due to the presence of negatively charged oxygen- containing groups. Besides, the nucleation overpotentials in Zn symmetric cells were also studied, and similar situation also occurs when deposited on the zinc matrix. Furthermore, the chronoamperometry (CA) test was carried out to study nucleation process and the growth of Zn2+ on zinc anode at overpotential of − 150 mV in symmetrical cells [49]. It is found that the current density in GQDs-free electrolyte presents a continuously increasing tendency within 200 s (Fig. 5c), suggesting a long and rampant planar diﬀusion process [50]. To minimize the surface energy, the absorbed ions diffuse laterally along the surface to accumulate on the tips and grow vertically into dendrites [51], In comparison to normal electrolyte, a stable and lower current density, is maintained after shorter 2D diffusion (within 100 s) in the presence of GQDs, suggesting that adsorbed Zn2+ seem to be locally reduced to Zn0 with the function of constant 3D diﬀusion. It may be because the adsorbed GQDs constrain 2D surface diffusion via coordination interaction between oxygen-contain groups (C-O and CO bonds) and Zn2+ ions. Therefore, adsorbed Zn2+ are guided to deposit adjacent region where the initial adsorption occurred, instead of the further area with lower surface energy, leading to generation of increased fine-size nucleation sites. The cross-section view of zinc anodes after 10 cycles at 1 mA cm−2 with capacity of 1 mAh cm−2 could be seen in Fig S16. As expected, Zn anode in normal electrolyte displays a rough surface consisting of irregular clusters while the surface in GQDs electrolyte maintains a compact Zn deposition layer without dendrites formation, which further elucidates the constraining of 2D surface diffusion in the presence of GQDs. Therefore, GQDs adsorbed on zinc anode could serve as nucleation site to reduce nucleation overpotential and enhance interfacial reaction kinetics, which is beneficial to suppress dendrite growth and reduce polarization voltage.

With the Tafel line extrapolation procedure on linear polarization curves in three-electrode system (Fig. 5d), low corrosion currents of 1.193 mA cm−2 is obtained for Zn foils in the presence of GQDs additive, whereas the Zn anode in 2 M ZnSO4 electrolyte shows a corrosion current of 2.558 mA cm−2, suggesting a reduced corrosion rate. Meanwhile, the stronger anti-corrosion ability also could be obtained in Zn||SS (stain steel) cells (Fig. S17). X-Ray Diffraction measurements were also performed to investigate the side reaction on the Zn electrode in Zn symmetric cells after continuous plating/stripping process (Figs. 5e and S18). In the ZnSO4 electrolyte, zinc anode presents a significant peak at about 8.5° at 0.8 mA cm−2 for 50 cycles, indexing to the (002) plane of Zn4SO4(OH)6.4 H2O by-product (PDF#44–0673) [52]. It can be seen that some new peaks located at 17.1° and 25.8° appeare after the side reactions of the Zn foil without GQDs electrolyte, attributing to the (004) and (006) planes of Zn4SO4(OH)6.4 H2O, respectively. This result strongly confirms that significant parasitic reaction occurred during repeated cycling. However, the peak intensity at about 8.5° appeared in the zinc anode become very weak and peaks at 17.1° and 25.8° are disappeared after inducing GQDs additive into electrolyte, which is ascribed to stronger interaction between GQDs and Zn2+. Moreover, the same process was repeated at the current density of 2.0 mA cm−2 (Fig. S18). The loose Zn4SO4(OH)6.4 H2O layer may block the ions and electronic transport at the solid-electrolyte interface, which further increases electrode polarization. Meanwhile, the ionic conductivity of electrolytes was also tested to study interactions of the ions and solvent. As shown in Fig S19, the electrolyte with GQDs shows significantly higher ionic conductivity of 5.97 mS cm–1 than that of pristine electrolyte (3.07 mS cm–1), indicating the restraining of ion-solvent cluster formation [53], which is in favor of reducing activity of water accounting for extended cycling lifetime and reduced polarization voltage. Besides, cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of Zn symmetric cells are shown in Fig. 5f. It is worth noting that peak area of redox reaction with GQDs is larger than that of GQDs-free electrolyte, indicating the higher electrochemical reactivity for Zn deposition [54].

To further investigate the validity of GQDs additive, Zn||NVO (NaxV2O5·nH2O) full cells were assembled where the NVO was prepared via modified one-step hydrothermal methods according to the reported literature. The XRD pattern of prepared NVO and corresponding SEM are shown in Fig. S20. Its CV curves present distinct redox peaks and no other redox peak appears when adding GQDs into electrolyte ( Fig. 6a), indicating the negligible effects of electrochemical inert GQDs on redox reaction of Zn||NVO batteries. In addition, after the first cycle, the following CV curves display more stable shapes and higher current density with GQDs additive in contrast to normal electrolyte, revealing more reversible electrochemical process and higher electrochemical reactivity. [17] To further verify the effectivity of GQDs on alleviating dendrites formation, SEM was carried out to observe the surface morphology of Zn anode after fifth cycles in Zn||NVO cells with or without GQDs (Fig. S21). There is no doubt that uneven zinc surface could easily been observed and numbers of dendrites aggregate together on the zinc anode in 2 M ZnSO4 electrolyte. In sharp contrast, flake-shaped Zn dendrites are greatly reduced and grow densely on the surface after addition of GQDs by virtue of homogeneous Zn2+ flux during plating/stripping process. Meanwhile, EIS measurements were carried out to investigate the influence of the GQDs additive on the inherent resistance of the ZIBs. As is shown in Figs. 6b and S22 , the full cells with GQDs additive present lower charge transfer resistance before cycling and after CV test (Table S4), suggesting that GQDs could effectively improve the ionic conductivity for fast redox reactions, which is in accordance with the conclusion in Zn symmetric cells. The full cells using GQDs electrolyte exhibit higher voltage plateau and discharge capacity than those of GQDs-free ZIBs at 50 mA g−1 (Fig. S23), which may be ascribed to its lower charge transfer resistance and alleviation of interfacial parasitic reaction. In addition, we further studied the cyclic stability of full cells with GQDs at low current density of 0.1 A g−1 and more stable cyclic performance could be seen from Fig. S24. Additionally, the long-term cycling performance of Zn||NVO full cells with GQDs at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 was tested, exhibiting much better cyclic stability. As shown in Fig. 6c, three is no distinct difference at the first and second cycle, however, rapid capacity fading was observed in the full cells without assistance of GQDs in next 10 cycles. Meanwhile, specific capacity of ZIBs without GQDs sharply dropped to 161.0 mAh g−1 after 500 cycles at 0.5 A g−1 (capacity retention of 58.12%). On the contrary, the full cells with GQDs exhibited superior cycling stability delivering much higher capacity of 192.1 mAh g−1 after 500 cycles (capacity retention of 70.32%). The selected discharge and charge capacity for the different cycles are exhibited in Fig. S25.

The rate performance of Zn||NVO full cells were estimated by a stepwise change in current densities between 0.1 and 2 A g−1. Impressively, the full cells with GQDs exhibit superior rate capability and reversibility with a capacity of 350 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 and 110 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1. Furthermore, when current density returned to 0.3 A g−1, the capacity increased to 260 mAh g−1 suggesting better cycling reversibility. For comparison, the cells in the absence of GQDs display awful rate performance and swift capacity deterioration, which is ascribed to the worse stability of zinc electrode and larger charge transfer resistance. The corresponding detailed capacity values of Zn||NVO full cells with and without GQDs at different current densities are presented in Fig. S26 . To evaluate the effect of GQDs on parasitic reactions, the open-circuit voltage attenuation of fully charged Zn||NVO cells was monitored. The cells were discharged to 0.4 V at 50 mA g−1 to investigate the coulombic efficiency. As shown in Figs. 6e, f and S27, more than 81% of the original capacity is retained and 1.17 V of open-circuit voltage after rest for 48 h is kept in the cell with GQDs additive，surpassing the 74.61% and 0.98 V for GQDs-free cell. This suggests that GQDs could validly suppress the interfacial side reactions such as formation of Zn4SO4(OH)6·xH2O and corrosion via preferential adsorption on zinc anode, which agrees well with the previous results. To further verify function of GQDs in improving cyclic stability at high current density, the cycling performance of full cells with GQDs-free and GQDs electrolyte at 1 A g−1 is exhibited in Fig. 6h. For GQDs-free electrolyte, the cell delivers unsatisfactory performance with quickly degraded capacity of 61.2 mAh g−1 after 600 cycles (capacity retention of 24.9%) and disappeared voltage plateau with severe polarization (Fig. S28). The cell with aid of GQDs presents more stable cycling performance with capacity of 164.3 mAh g−1 (capacity retention of 68.03%). The cycling performance over 500 cycles at higher current density of 2 A g−1 and loading of the active material were also investigated (Fig. S29). As expected, a superior capacity retention of 77.2% when introducing GQDs as additive demonstrates the excellent cycling stability of zinc anode due to inhibition of dendrite formation and side reactions. The enhanced cycling reversibility of the full cell on account of suppression of dendrites formation, side reaction and improvement of ion conductivity is in agreement with improved performance of half cells during repeated cycling process, which further confirms the promising application of GQDs in ZIBs.

On basis of above-mentioned analyses of experiment, the GQDs with oxygen-containing functional groups (C-O and CO bonds) and good water solubility as electrolyte additive could effectively suppress the growth of Zn dendrites, and then significantly improve cycling performance and reversibility of Zn anode. Supported by DFT calculation and CLSM observation, the stronger coordination interactions between GQDs and Zn2+ ions lead to the fact that, the adsorbed GQDs on the zinc anode and dissolved GQDs in electrolytes can validly promote homogeneous Zn2+ ions distribution and accelerate the kinetics of Zn2+ deposition during plating/stripping process. Therefore, when GQDs (0.4 g L−1) are added into electrolyte solution, zinc anode exhibit a long-term cycling lifetime up to 2200 h at 0.8 mA cm−2 and a lower polarization voltage of 50 mV with smooth zinc anode surface. Furthermore, the zinc ion batteries assembled by NVO, Zn metal, and electrolyte with GQDs show excellent cycling stability and rate performance, at the current density of 1 A g−1, the reversible specific capacity can be still maintained at 164.3 mAh g−1 after 600 cycles, which is much higher than that (61.2 mAh g−1) of contrast sample without GQDs. This work presents a facile and effective strategy to restrain zinc dendritic formation and growth for aqueous ZIBs in mild acidic electrolyte, which may facilitate the development of aqueous ZIBs and graphene quantum dots.

Hao Zhang: Investigation, Experiment, Software, Writing − original draft. Ruiting Guo: Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization. Shuo Li: Validation, Formal analysis. Cheng Liu: Formal analysis. Haoyang Li: Formal analysis. Jiugang Hu: Formal analysis. Guoqiang Zou: Writing − review & editing. Hongshuai Hou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing − review & editing, Supervision. Xiaobo Ji: Writing − review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
